chunks.text: DNA Profiling & Population Frequency"I have good news and bad news," a defense attorney told his client. "First the bad news. The blood test came back, and your DNA is an exact match with the sample found on the victim's dress.""Oh, no - I'm ruined! What's the good news?" cried the client."Your cholesterol is down to 140," said the defense attorney.Making the conclusion that a DNA sample matches another DNA sample is not a quick or simple process. It is done only after careful and rigorous analysis of both DNA samples and after a comparison to the general population is made using probability ratios involving population frequency.Validating a DNA Match Using Population Frequency ValuesHuman chromosomes have so much DNA that to match crime scene DNA samples to suspect DNA samples forensic experts must focus upon certain sections of DNA that are unique and distinctive. The unique segments of DNA used are known as the VNTR and STR fragments. To ensure further that the DNA analysis is accurate, VNTR or STR sections from five different loci are compared. Even so, there is still a chance that a certain VNTR sequence or STR sequence can be found randomly in more than one person. Forensic experts acknowledge this fact, and with each DNA match a related probability is included. The probability given is the frequency of this DNA sequence occurring in the general population; this is known as population frequency.Population frequency values are presented with each DNA match because a data bank of all human DNA does not exist. If a sample of the DNA of every person in the world or even a country did exist, there would be no need to include a population frequency value with a match. Instead, the match would be undisputable. Some nations have begun DNA data banks for their respective countries containing DNA from convicted criminals.The probability range for RFLP data is high - one in hundreds of thousands or even one in a million. This is because RFLP focuses upon the longer VNTR fragments that contain numerous nucleic acid bases. The more nucleic acid bases in a DNA sample, the less likely an identical match to this sequence will be found. However, now that the RFLP method has been in use for over 20 years, the occurance of laboratory interpretation errors is fairly high. Therefore, this method is being used less frequently in forensic investigations.The PCR method of DNA analysis uses STR sequences. The probability range of a PCR analysis tends to be somewhat lower than RFLP analysis because of the smaller size of the STR DNA fragments used. The advantage of PCR analysis is that the occurence of laboratory interpretation errors is significantly less in comparison to RFLP analysis. However, any small contamination of the evidence collected or contamination of any of the lab equipment used leads to false results. Although the probability range is lower than RFLP analysis, PCR analysis has steadily become the preferred choice of DNA profiling in forensic investigations because of its ability to analyze small amounts of DNA evidence in a short period of time with less error.In 1989, Allan Legere was serving a life sentence in prison for murder. He escaped while he was at the hospital being treated for an ear infection. In his seven months of freedom, he killed four people, sexually assaulted three women, and committed one act of arson. He became the first Canadian to be convicted using DNA evidence in 1991.
